result.claim: The pipeline converts noisy dataset tags into a compact, human‑readable domain palette using embedding and clustering techniques.
result.original_text: conversion of noisy dataset tags into a compact, human‑readable domain palette via the utilisation of embedding and clustering techniques

result.claim: The approach uses structured dataset samples (column names with optional rows) with zero‑, one‑, and few‑shot prompting strategies to assign domains.
result.original_text: leverages structured dataset samples (column names with optional rows) under zero-, one-, and few-shot prompting strategies to assign domains

result.claim: Classical baselines evaluated include term/tag overlap, column‑name embedding classifiers, and meta‑characteristics models.
result.original_text: classical baselines – term/tag overlap, column-name embedding classifiers, and meta-characteristics models
result.value: term/tag overlap; column-name embedding classifiers; meta-characteristics models

result.claim: In few‑shot settings, modern large language models are competitive with classical baselines.
result.original_text: modern LLMs demonstrate competitiveness in few-shot settings

result.claim: Incorporating row samples together with careful demonstration selection improves the robustness of the system.
result.original_text: incorporation of row samples, in conjunction with judicious demonstration choice, serves to enhance the robustness of the system

result.claim: A benchmark of diverse tabular datasets was constructed for evaluation.
result.original_text: constructed benchmark of diverse tabular datasets

result.claim: The code and data for the pipeline are released to enable reproducibility.
Early methodologies used handcrafted rules, dictionaries, and regular expressions to characterize column semantics such as detecting ZIP codes and dates.
result.original_text: Early methodologies for table understanding approached via handcrafted rules, dictionaries, and regular expressions to characterize column semantics (e.g., detecting ZIP codes, dates).

result.claim: Sherlock integrated embeddings of cell values, column headers, and character‑level features into a deep semantic type model.
result.original_text: Sherlock ... integrated embeddings of cell values, headers of columns, and character-level features into a deep semantic type model.
result.value: embeddings of cell values, headers, character-level features

result.claim: The D4 framework discovers latent domains by learning contextual embeddings of column values and clustering them across large corpora.
result.original_text: The framework D4 for discovering latent domains by learning contextual embeddings of column values and clustering them across large corpora was introduced.
result.value: contextual embeddings of column values, clustering

result.claim: Sato combined neural models with table context features to improve robustness on messy enterprise data.
result.original_text: Sato combined neural models with table context features, resulting in robustness on messy enterprise data.
result.value: neural models with table context features

result.claim: Kepler‑aSI combined machine learning, knowledge‑graph lookups, and human‑in‑the‑loop annotation to create semantic descriptions of datasets.
result.original_text: Practical annotation tool Kepler-aSI combined machine learning, knowledge-graph lookups, and human-in-the-loop annotation to create semantic descriptions of datasets.
result.value: machine learning, knowledge-graph lookups, human-in-the-loop annotation

result.claim: A tag embedding function maps tags from the vocabulary to a p‑dimensional vector space.
result.original_text: We fix a tag embedding function φ: V → ℝ^p

result.claim: Dataset‑level embedding is computed by averaging the embeddings of its tags.
result.original_text: obtain a dataset-level embedding by aggregating the tag embeddings, e.g. by averaging: z_i = (1/m_i) Σ φ(t_ij)

result.claim: A clustering algorithm is applied to the matrix of dataset embeddings to produce K cluster assignments.
result.original_text: we apply a clustering algorithm C: ℝ^{N×p} → {1,…,K}^N to obtain cluster assignments y_i = C(Z)_i

result.claim: Tags are short human‑provided textual labels from OpenML used to annotate datasets, tasks, and benchmarks.
result.original_text: tags are short human-provided textual labels used in OpenML to annotate datasets, tasks, and benchmarks

result.claim: Meta‑characteristics baseline accuracy is 0.44 ± 0.03.
Meta‑characteristics baseline Top‑3 recall is 0.71 ± 0.00.
Meta‑characteristics baseline F1‑score is 0.41 ± 0.02.
D4 baseline accuracy is 0.27 ± 0.06.
D4 baseline Top‑3 recall is 0.43 ± 0.07.
D4 baseline F1‑score is 0.24 ± 0.07.
Embeddings of column names baseline accuracy is 0.61 ± 0.04.
Embeddings of column names baseline Top‑3 recall is 0.82 ± 0.02.
Embeddings of column names baseline F1‑score is 0.59 ± 0.04.
gpt‑4o zero‑shot accuracy is 0.61 ± 0.03.
gpt‑4o one‑shot accuracy is 0.57 ± 0.01.
gpt‑4o few‑shot accuracy is 0.63 ± 0.01.
gpt‑4o zero‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.72 ± 0.04.
gpt‑4o one‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.59 ± 0.01.
gpt‑4o few‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.63 ± 0.01.
gpt‑4o zero‑shot F1‑score is 0.57 ± 0.05.
gpt‑4o one‑shot F1‑score is 0.52 ± 0.01.
gpt‑4o few‑shot F1‑score is 0.58 ± 0.01.
DeepSeek zero‑shot accuracy is 0.55 ± 0.05.
DeepSeek one‑shot accuracy is 0.55 ± 0.01.
DeepSeek few‑shot accuracy is 0.62 ± 0.02.
DeepSeek zero‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.72 ± 0.10.
DeepSeek one‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.72 ± 0.01.
DeepSeek few‑shot Top‑3 recall is 0.81 ± 0.03.
When using only column headers, gpt‑4o few‑shot accuracy increases to 0.64.
When using only column headers, gpt‑4o few‑shot macro F1‑score increases to 0.60.
When using only column headers, DeepSeek few‑shot accuracy is 0.62.
When using only column headers, DeepSeek few‑shot F1‑score is 0.56.
Using 5‑row samples generally yields slightly higher or comparable performance than using 50‑row samples.
result.original_text: bars corresponding to 5-row samples are generally slightly higher than or comparable to those for 50-row samples

result.claim: For domains like Medical Science, Finance & Economics, Environmental Science, Social Sciences, and Transportation Systems, F1‑scores often exceed 0.8.
For domains like Biology & Genetics, Synthetic Systems, and Veterinary Science, F1‑scores collapse to zero.
For the Computer Science & Artificial Intelligence domain, gpt‑4o F1‑score is close to zero, while DeepSeek achieves 0.40.
result.original_text: gpt‑4o ... close to zero, DeepSeek achieves a modest 0.40 F1

result.claim: The initial data collection sourced roughly 100 tagged tabular datasets from OpenML.
result.original_text: The initial data collection involved sourcing roughly 100 tagged tabular datasets from OpenML.

result.claim: Each dataset was downloaded as a pandas DataFrame and stored as full_dataset.csv in a dedicated directory.
result.original_text: Each dataset was downloaded as a pandas DataFrame and stored as full_dataset.csv in a dedicated directory.

result.claim: Metadata (dataset ID, name, tags, feature descriptions, row count) was serialized to metadata.json.
result.original_text: the associated metadata (dataset ID, name, tags, feature descriptions, and row count) was serialized to metadata.json

result.claim: All tags were aggregated into a global vocabulary and saved in unique_tags.json.
result.original_text: All tags attached to these datasets were aggregated into a global vocabulary and saved in unique_tags.json

result.claim: Tag vocabulary was embedded using Sentence Transformers (all-mpnet-base-v2).
Tag vocabulary was embedded using TF‑IDF.
Tag vocabulary was embedded using Word2Vec.
Clustering algorithms HDBSCAN, k‑means, agglomerative clustering, and DBSCAN were applied with fixed hyperparameters.
result.original_text: we apply a suite of clustering algorithms—HDBSCAN, k‑means, agglomerative clustering, and DBSCAN—withfixed hyperparameters

result.claim: Silhouette score and Davies‑Bouldin score were computed for each embedding‑clustering configuration.
result.original_text: we compute internal validity indices, namely the Silhouette score and the Davies—Bouldin score

result.claim: Sentence Transformers with HDBSCAN produced 8 clusters, Silhouette 0.052, Davies‑Bouldin 2.675.
Sentence Transformers with KMEANS produced 8 clusters, Silhouette 0.044, Davies‑Bouldin 2.897.
Sentence Transformers with AGGLOMERATIVE produced 14 clusters, Silhouette 0.106, Davies‑Bouldin 1.781.
Sentence Transformers with DBSCAN produced 2 clusters, Silhouette 0.931, Davies‑Bouldin 3.005.
TFIDF with HDBSCAN produced 8 clusters, Silhouette 0.040, Davies‑Bouldin 4.127.
TFIDF with KMEANS produced 8 clusters, Silhouette 0.073, Davies‑Bouldin 1.914.
TFIDF with AGGLOMERATIVE produced 15 clusters, Silhouette 0.131, Davies‑Bouldin 1.749.
TFIDF with DBSCAN produced 2 clusters, Silhouette 0.025, Davies‑Bouldin 1.699.
Word2Vec with HDBSCAN produced 5 clusters, Silhouette 0.014, Davies‑Bouldin 4.062.
Word2Vec with KMEANS produced 6 clusters, Silhouette 0.010, Davies‑Bouldin 3.640.
Word2Vec with AGGLOMERATIVE produced 15 clusters, Silhouette 0.016, Davies‑Bouldin 2.414.
Word2Vec with DBSCAN produced 4 clusters, Silhouette 0.021, Davies‑Bouldin 4.126.
Agglomerative clustering in the Sentence Transformer embedding space was selected as the basis for defining the final set of domains.
result.original_text: we selected agglomerative clustering in the Sentence Transformer embedding space as the basis for defining the final set of domains

result.claim: The chosen clustering of tag embeddings induces 14 semantic domains.
result.original_text: The chosen clustering of tag embeddings induces 14 semantic domains

result.claim: The final benchmark comprises 258 tabular datasets distributed across 14 domains.
result.original_text: The final benchmark, after this enrichment step, comprises 258 tabular datasets distributed across 14 domains

result.claim: Clusters with fewer than five datasets were enriched by querying OpenML for up to ten additional datasets per domain.
result.original_text: clusters with fewer than five datasets were enriched by querying OpenML for up to ten additional datasets per domain
result.value: up to ten additional datasets per domain

result.claim: The benchmark is partitioned into a training index set and a test index set.
result.original_text: The benchmark is partitioned into a training index set \(\mathcal{I}_{\text{train}}\) and a test index set \(\mathcal{I}_{\text{test}}\).

result.claim: Five independent stratified splits are created with 20% of the datasets assigned to the test set for each split.
result.original_text: we perform 5 independent stratified splits ... with |\mathcal{F}_{\text{test}}^{(s)}|/N = 0.2 and stratification preserving the empirical domain distribution.

result.claim: All experimental runs use temperature = 0.3 and maximum tokens = 500 for decoding.
result.original_text: shared decoding settings (e.g., temperature = 0.3, maximum tokens = 500) fixed across models and modes.

result.claim: The large language models evaluated are DeepSeek and gpt‑4o.
Accuracy is computed as the proportion of test datasets where the primary prediction matches the ground‑truth domain.
result.original_text: Accuracy is computed as the proportion of test datasets for which the primary prediction matches the ground‑truth domain.

result.claim: Top‑3 Recall measures the proportion of test datasets where the true domain appears in any of the up to three predicted labels.
result.original_text: Top‑3 Recall measures the proportion of test datasets for which the true domain appears in any of the up to three predicted labels.

result.claim: Macro F1‑score is computed by averaging per‑domain F1 scores derived from the primary predictions across all domains.
result.original_text: Macro F1‑score is computed by first deriving per‑domain precision and recall from the primary predictions, then averaging the F1‑scores uniformly over all domains.

result.claim: The subsampling operator selects all column headers and the first 5 data rows, formatted as a structured table.
result.original_text: selects all column headers and the first 5 data rows, formatted as a structured table (e.g., Markdown-like).

result.claim: Zero‑shot uses no in‑context examples, one‑shot uses one example, and few‑shot uses five examples.
Example sets are sampled uniformly at random from the training split.
result.original_text: choosing the elements of S^{(s,m)} uniformly at random

result.claim: The subsampling operator r selects a finite collection of rows, typically a few dozen rows of the table.
result.original_text: r that selects a finite collection of rows X_i = r(D_i) (e.g., a few dozen rows of the table).

result.claim: The headers‑only representation consists solely of the list of column names formatted as a simple schema.
result.original_text: Headers-only representation ... is a textual object consisting only of the list of column names, formatted as a simple schema.

result.claim: The in‑context regimes evaluated are zero‑shot, one‑shot, and few‑shot corresponding to m values 0, 1, and 5.
result.original_text: for each in‑context regime m ∈ {0, 1, 5} (zero‑shot, one‑shot, few‑shot)

result.claim: The number of labeled examples per regime is 0 for zero‑shot, 1 for one‑shot, and 5 for few‑shot.
The evaluation metrics computed are Accuracy, Top‑3 Recall, and macro F1‑score.
result.original_text: we compute Accuracy (primary label), Top‑3 Recall (primary + up to two alternatives), and macro F1‑score per split

result.claim: Results are aggregated across the five stratified splits by reporting mean and standard deviation for each metric, representation type, LLM, and in‑context regime.
result.original_text: aggregate results across the 5 stratified splits by reporting the mean and standard deviation for each metric, representation type, LLM, and in‑context regime.
result.value: mean and standard deviation across 5 splits

result.claim: The same decoding parameters (temperature, maximum length, etc.) are used for all prompts.
result.original_text: using the same decoding parameters (temperature, maximum length, etc.)

result.claim: The prompt construction function Π is identical to that used in the main LLM experiments.
result.original_text: Π is the same prompt-construction function used in the main LLM experiments
